title: First aid kit for a smallholding
subtitle: What to have on hand for livestock emergencies before the vet arrives.
excerpt: A well-stocked farm medicine cabinet lets you stabilise an animal and make the vet's job easier. Most essentials are not prescription items and can be bought in advance. This covers what to stock, what requires a vet prescription, and the critical distinction between what you treat yourself and what you call the vet for.
sourceNotes: Farmers Weekly, farm first aid kit guide. DEFRA livestock welfare guidance. SAC Consulting Scotland, flock health planning. British Veterinary Association, on-farm medicine storage guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Veterinary medicines that can only be supplied or administered under the direction of a vet. Most injectable antibiotics, certain antiparasitic treatments, and prescription painkillers are POM-V. They cannot be bought over the counter.

The minimum time between the last dose of a medicine and the date an animal can legally be slaughtered for food or its milk used for human consumption. Must be recorded in the medicine book for all medicines given to food-producing animals.

content.text: A well-stocked farm medicine cabinet is assembled before emergencies happen, not during them. The items below can be sourced from an agricultural merchant or your vet; some require a vet relationship to obtain. Establish a vet for your livestock before you need one; some vets do not take on new farm clients for emergencies only.

Disposable gloves (sizes L and XL). Sterile gauze and bandaging material. Iodine solution (7% teat dip or wound spray) and purple spray (antiseptic wound marker). Clinical thermometer. Colostrum supplement sachets for lambs and kids. Electrolyte powder sachets for oral rehydration. A stomach tube and dose syringe. A drenching gun. Lubricating gel for obstetric work. A lamb and kid stomach tube set.
Injectable calcium borogluconate (for milk fever in sheep and goats), injectable magnesium sulphate (for grass staggers), antibiotic spray, and oxytocin are all

items. A vet can prescribe a small stock of commonly needed items under a standing prescription for your holding if you demonstrate knowledge of their use and record-keeping. All POM-V use must be recorded with the

Conditions that require a vet rather than first aid: suspected broken bones or internal injuries, prolapsed uterus, any animal that is collapsed and unresponsive, birthing difficulties that have not resolved after 30 minutes of active straining, and any notifiable disease (if in doubt, call the vet before touching the animal). The purpose of a first aid cabinet is to stabilise and support until professional help arrives, not to replace it.
